coredata.dc:description: Bacterial resistance to antibiotics has rendered bacterial infections an escalating threat to public health, prompting numerous researchers to strive towards the development of novel antimicrobial agents. Designable ionic liquids (ILs) have gained increasing prominence in the field of antibacterials. However, certain ILs exhibit suboptimal antimicrobial activity. In this study, we synthesized ionic liquid-derived carbon dots (IL-CDs: VOIMBr-CD) through vinyl and epoxy group reactions, resulting in enhanced antimicrobial efficacy. The VOIMBr-CD retains the antibacterial structure characteristic of ILs, including a positively charged imidazolium group and long alkyl chain. Moreover, VOIMBr-CD demonstrates selective antibacterial action against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) while effectively inhibiting S. aureus biofilm growth. Notably, the antibacterial efficacy of VOIMBr-CD against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), surpasses vancomycin (Van). We conducted comprehensive investigations into the selective antibacterial mechanism of VOIMBr-CD which primarily involves electrostatic interactions, hydrophobic interactions, as well as generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) within bacteria cells. In vivo experiments demonstrated that VOIMBr-CD significantly alleviate inflammation, facilitated collagen deposition and angiogenesis, and effectively expedited wound healing in MRSA-infected mice models; thus, highlighting their potential as therapeutic candidates for diseases resembling MRSA infection-like conditions. Importantly, this work presents a novel approach utilizing IL-based materials for developing effective antibacterials thereby offering new insights into the application prospects of IL-derived materials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of VOIMBr-CD . (a) TEM image (inset: grain size distribution histogram and HRTEM image). (b) XRD pattern. (c) FT-IR spectra. (d) XPS total curve. (e) C1s, (f) N1s, (g) O1s high-resolution XPS curves. (h) UV–vis absorption spectrum. (i) Excitation and emission spectra (Illustration: VOIMBr-CD solution irradiated by visible light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right)).

In vitro antibacterial performance of VOIMBr-CD. The dose-response relationship of VOIMBr-CD against E. coli (a), P. aeruginosa (b) and B. subtilis (c). (d) Colony survival of E. coli and S. aureus treated with VOIMBr-CD at different concentrations. The colony formation outcomes (e) and the corresponding statistical histogram (f) of S. aureus following a 2 h exposure to VOIMBr-CD, GTA, and VOIMBr at a concentration of 20 µg/mL. (g) Bactericidal kinetics curves of VOIMBr-CD with different concentrations on S. aureus. (h) Statistical histogram comparing the survival rate of S. aureus treated with Jieyoushen antibacterial spray and VOIMBr-CD at the same concentration. (i) Statistical histogram comparing the survival rate of MRSA treated with Van and VOIMBr-CD at the same concentration. (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01).

In vitro anti-biofilm activity of VOIMBr-CD. (a) Inhibitory rate of S. aureus biofilms by different concentrations of VOIMBr-CD (crystal violet assay). Crystal violet staining photos (b) and plate photos (c) of S. aureus biofilm under different concentrations of VOIMBr-CD. (d) SEM and CLSM images of S. aureus biofilms before and after treatment with VOIMBr-CD.

Selective antibacterial mechanism of VOIMBr-CD. (a) Zeta potentials of VOIMBr-CD and of E. coli and S. aureus before and after their interaction with VOIMBr-CD. (b) CLSM images of S. aureus and E. coli incubated with VOIMBr-CD. SEM photographs (c) and images of Live/dead bacteria staining (d) of E. coli and S. aureus before and after interaction with VOIMBr-CD.

In vivo antibacterial activity of VOIMBr-CD. (a) Schematic representation of an animal model of MRSA-infected wounds and the wound healing process under VOIMBr-CD treatment. (b) Representative images of MRSA-infected wounds at different time points from four groups. (c) Wound healing rates of four groups at 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 and 12 days of wound treatment. (d) Statistical histograms of wound tissue colonies on the 12th day of wound treatment (used 5 mice for experiments and used 3 mice for counting). (e) Images of colonies of wound tissue homogenate from four groups on days 0 and 12 of wound treatment. (f) Body weight change curves of mice in the four groups. (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01).

H&E staining, Masson staining, and immunohistochemical analysis. The H&E staining results (a), Masson staining results (b), IL-6 staining results (c), TNF-α staining results (d), VEGF staining results (e), rate of collagen fiber area (f), IL-6 expression level (g), TNF-α expression level (h) and VEGF expression level (i) of the four groups of wound tissues at 12 days of wound treatment. (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01).

In vitro and in vivo biocompatibility of VOIMBr-CD. Cell viability of HeLa cells (a) and hemolytic activity on mouse erythrocytes (b) after treated with different concentrations of VOIMBr-CD. (c) H&E staining results of heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney after 12 days of wound treatment in four groups of mice.
The synthesis process of VOIMBr-CD and application of VOIMBr-CD in wound healing and inhibition of biofilm formation.

head.ce:title: Ionic liquid-derived carbon dots with enhanced antibacterial activity and antibiofilm capability for treatment of MRSA-infected wounds

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial resistance to antibiotics has rendered bacterial infections an escalating threat to public health, prompting numerous researchers to strive towards the development of novel antimicrobial agents. Designable ionic liquids (ILs) have gained increasing prominence in the field of antibacterials. However, certain ILs exhibit suboptimal antimicrobial activity. In this study, we synthesized ionic liquid-derived carbon dots (IL-CDs: VOIMBr-CD) through vinyl and epoxy group reactions, resulting in enhanced antimicrobial efficacy. The VOIMBr-CD retains the antibacterial structure characteristic of ILs, including a positively charged imidazolium group and long alkyl chain. Moreover, VOIMBr-CD demonstrates selective antibacterial action against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) while effectively inhibiting S. aureus biofilm growth. Notably, the antibacterial efficacy of VOIMBr-CD against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), surpasses vancomycin (Van). We conducted comprehensive investigations into the selective antibacterial mechanism of VOIMBr-CD which primarily involves electrostatic interactions, hydrophobic interactions, as well as generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) within bacteria cells. In vivo experiments demonstrated that VOIMBr-CD significantly alleviate inflammation, facilitated collagen deposition and angiogenesis, and effectively expedited wound healing in MRSA-infected mice models; thus, highlighting their potential as therapeutic candidates for diseases resembling MRSA infection-like conditions. Importantly, this work presents a novel approach utilizing IL-based materials for developing effective antibacterials thereby offering new insights into the application prospects of IL-derived materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Healthcare practitioners have extensively utilized antibiotics in recent decades to treat illnesses caused by bacterial infections. The emergence and rapid dissemination of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) has led to a renewed recognition of bacterial infection-related disorders as a significant global health concern [1,2]. Bacterial biofilm represents the primary form of bacterial infection [3]. Research studies have demonstrated that approximately 80 % of bacterial infections are attributed to biofilms [4], including infections associated with implanted medical devices [5]. Bacterial biofilms are microbial aggregates comprising bacteria embedded within a self-produced extracellular matrix (EPS) [6,7]. Biofilm formation can facilitate the accumulation of antibiotic resistance genes, thereby promoting the development of antibiotic resistance [8]. Moreover, bacterial infections possess the potential to impede wound healing and give rise to complications such as multiple organ failure and sepsis, occasionally leading to fatal outcomes in extreme cases [9–11]. Consequently, it is imperative to discover novel antimicrobial agents for effectively addressing bacterial infectious diseases and mitigating or reversing the crisis posed by antibacterial resistance.

Rapid advancements in nanoscience and technology present promising alternatives to antimicrobial therapy [12]. In contrast to conventional antibiotics derived from microorganisms or natural products, nanomaterials possess unique physicochemical properties that circumvent existing mechanisms of acquired resistance and do not readily induce bacterial resistance [13]. A diverse range of functional nanoparticles with antimicrobial properties have recently been developed, including liposomes [14], metal nanomaterials [15,16], polymer nanomaterials [17,18], and carbon-based nanomaterials [19,20]. Carbon dots (CDs), a novel carbon-based nanomaterial characterized by their small nanoscale size, which facilitates cellular uptake, convenient surface modification [21], abundant raw material sources enabling modulation of CDs properties through suitable precursor selection [22], excellent fluorescence properties [23], and good biocompatibility [24], have found extensive applications in bioimaging [25–27], biosensing [28,29], drug delivery [30–32], anticancer therapy [33–35] and antimicrobial therapy [36–38]. The antimicrobial activity of CDs is primarily attributed to their particle size, surface functional groups, and surface charge characteristics [39]. Currently, the mechanisms underlying the antimicrobial effects of CDs can be categorized into several aspects: generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) via photocatalytic or non-photocatalytic processes; disruption of cell membrane structures through electrostatic or hydrophobic interactions; disruption of DNA structures [38,40–42].

CDs synthesized from antimicrobial molecules as precursors typically retain the active antimicrobial groups of the precursors and show better antimicrobial activities than raw materials, possibly due to positive charge changes or enhanced activity of the antimicrobial groups. However, it is important to consider whether CDs can maintain their precursor's antimicrobial groups after undergoing high temperature and pressure reactions. In recent years, CDs derived from various antibacterial compounds such as natural drugs, antibiotics, and quaternary ammonium salts have been widely utilized for antibacterial purposes and wound healing in bacterial infections. To enhance the biological activity of curcumin, excellent broad-spectrum antibacterial CDs were synthesized using a “double-heat” method with 2,3-epoxypropyltrimethylammonium chloride (GTA) and natural curcumin [43]. One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of levofloxacin-based CDs retained levofloxacin's antibacterial group to achieve dual bactericidal effects based on positive charge generation and ROS production [44]. The CDs prepared from glycerol- and dimethyloctadecyl[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ammonium chloride-derived carbon source interacted with bacterial cells carrying negative charges through electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions [41].

Ionic liquids (ILs), which can be customized by selecting synthetic unit structures, are increasingly significant in the research and development of non-antibiotic therapies [45]. Based on the given information, it is possible that retaining the bactericidal group of ILs in ionic liquid-derived carbon dots (IL-CDs) contributes to their improved antibacterial activities. Currently, IL-CDs have limited applications related to antimicrobial use, with their main application areas being sensing [46], catalysis [47] and bioimaging [48]. In this study, we designed and synthesized novel IL-CDs (VOIMBr-CD) using the reaction (vinyl and epoxy groups) of 1-octyl-3-vinylimidazolium bromide (VOIMBr) and GTA to fully preserve the antimicrobial structure of antimicrobial ILs for superior properties (Scheme 1 ). Characterization results revealed that VOIMBr-CD retain key antimicrobial structures of VOIMBr: alkyl chains and positively charged imidazolium groups, providing enhanced selective antimicrobial activity. Compared to vancomycin (Van), VOIMBr-CD exhibited superior antibacterial efficacy against on methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). The antibacterial mechanisms include: (1) positive VOIMBr-CD selectively adhering to Gram-positive bacteria-Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) with more anion sites through electrostatic action; (2) increased membrane permeability due to long alkyl chains on the surface; (3) induction of elevated ROS levels in bacteria by VOIMBr-CD resulting in bacterial death. Furthermore, VOIMBr-CD manifested remarkable in vivo antibacterial activity, facilitated collagen deposition and angiogenesis, and efficaciously expedited the healing of MRSA-infected wound.

1-Vinylimidazole, GTA and Van were obtained from Maclean Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Bromooctane and agar were purchased from Aladdin Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Disodium hydrogen phosphate, potassium chloride, sodium chloride, and potassium dihydrogen phosphate were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Yeast extract and tryptone were procured from Solarbio Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The Jieyoushen antibacterial spray was purchased from Jingdong. The live/dead bacteria staining kit (L6060, Nucgreen/EthD III) was supplied by Warid Technology Ltd. (Beijing, China). The ROS Assay Kit (dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate, DCFH-DA) was provided by Jebis Biotechnology Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). The dialysis bag with a MWCO of 1000 Da was supplied by Vokai Technologies Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Both S. aureus (ATCC 25923) and Escherichia coli (ATCC 25922, E. coli) were acquired from An Xin Kang Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Pseudomonas aeruginosa (ATCC 15442, P. aeruginosa), Bacillus subtilis (ATCC 6633, B. subtilis) and MRSA (ATCC 43300) were provided by Baiou Bowei Bio Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The chemical reagents used were of analytical grade and did not necessitate purification. All solutions required for the experiment were prepared with deionized water.

At room temperature, 1-vinylimidazole (0.025 mol) and bromooctane (0.025 mol) were stirred for 72 h to produce VOIMBr. After three ether and ethyl acetate washes, the product was dried for 48 h at 50 °C in a vacuum oven [49]. 1H NMR ( Fig. S1 ) showed that the purity of VOIMBr is relatively high with no obvious impurity peaks. 1H NMR (DMSO‑d 6, 400 MHz, ppm): 9.91(s, 1H), 8.34(s, 1H), 8.06(s, 1H), 7.37(m, 1H), 6.02(dd, 1H), 5.37(dd, 1H), 4.26(t, 2H), 1.79(m, 2H), 1.19(m, 10H), 0.74(t, 3H).

VOIMBr-CD were synthesized by a one-pot method using GTA and VOIMBr as reaction precursors by reference to the preparation method in the literature [50]. 1 mL of deionized water was mixed with precisely weighed 0.1 g of GTA, and the above solution was transferred to a round-bottomed flask and stirred at 170 °C in an open state (No reflux device is requisite) for 20 min. Then 1 mL of VOIMBr aqueous solution (0.2 g/mL) was added and the reaction was continued at 170 °C for 40 min with stirring (nGTA: nIL=1:1). After cooling to room temperature, a dark brown flocculent was obtained at the bottom of the round-bottomed flask and 3 mL of deionized water was added to dissolve the flocculent by ultrasonication. After centrifugation for 10 min (5000 rpm), the dark brown liquid obtained was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane, and then was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MW: 1000) for 24 h, changing the water every 2 h. To obtain VOIMBr-CD solid powder, the dialyzed liquid was freeze-dried.

The synthesis steps of GTA-CD were as follows: 2 mL of deionized water was mixed with precisely weighed 0.1 g of GTA, and the above solution was transferred to a round-bottomed flask and stirred at 170 °C in an open state (No reflux device is requisite) for 60 min. The subsequent steps were the same as VOIMBr-CD.

The morphology of VOIMBr-CD was examined by Tecnai G2 TF20 transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectra were utilized for analyze the elemental and chemical bond composition of VOIMBr-CD were recorded by ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermoelectric Corporation, USA). IFS120HR Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Bruker, Germany) was used to measure the infrared spectra. 6100 X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Shimadzu, Japan) was applied to characterize the crystallinity of VOIMBr-CD. A Shimadzu-3600 UV–Vis spectrophotometer and a HORIBA Fluoomax-4 fluorescence (FL) spectrometer provided UV–Vis spectra and FL spectra, respectively.

First, the strain (E. coli, S. aureus, MRSA, P. aeruginosa or B. subtilis) was removed from the −40 °C ultra-low temperature refrigerator. After thawing in a 4 °C refrigerator, 10 µL of the bacterial suspension were placed on a sterilized LB agar plates and cultivated for 16 h. After that, a monoclonal colony was picked from the LB agar plates using a sterilized inoculation loop, injected into liquid LB medium and cultured overnight in a constant temperature oscillator at 37 °C to obtain a preliminary bacterial suspension. The bacterial suspension was then re-inoculated 1:1000 into fresh LB liquid medium and cultured until the optical density at 600 nm (OD600) reached 0.6-0.8, which was diluted for subsequent experiments.

Germ-free PBS (0.01 M, pH=7.4) was used to dilute the bacterial suspensions to 106-107 CFU/mL (S. aureus; E. coli; MRSA; P. aeruginosa; B. subtilis). The broad-spectrum antibacterial performance of VOIMBr-CD was evaluated by plate counting method. The diluted bacterial suspensions were mixed with equal volumes of different concentrations of VOIMBr-CD solutions and incubated on a shaker (37 °C, 180 rpm) for 2 h. After incubation, 100 μL of the 1000-fold diluted mixture were spread onto LB agar plates and cultivated upside down at 37 °C for 20-24 h to record the colony growth.

The antibacterial effect of VOIMBr-CD and commercial antibacterial spray (Jieyoushen antibacterial spray) on S. aureus was compared using the plate counting method. VOIMBr-CD and commercial antibacterial sprays with different concentration gradients PBS solution were co-incubated with S. aureus bacterial PBS suspension (106-107 CFU/mL) for 2 h, and the process for diluting the agar plates was identical to the one above.

Further, the antibacterial effect of VOIMBr-CD and Van on MRSA was also compared using the plate counting method. VOIMBr-CD and Van with different concentration gradients PBS solution were co-incubated with MRSA bacterial PBS suspension (106-107 CFU/mL) for 2 h, and the process for diluting the agar plates was identical to the one above.

The 107-108 CFU/mL bacterial PBS suspensions were grown for 2h at 37 °C with various VOIMBr-CD concentrations PBS solution. The bacterial suspensions that had been exposed to various VOIMBr-CD concentrations were then transferred to LB liquid medium and incubated in a shaking apparatus. Several samples were taken at various times (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 and 12 h) during the culture phase in order to measure and record the OD600.

To assess VOIMBr-CD rapid bactericidal potential, the bactericidal effect in different incubation durations was examined. Equal amounts of various VOIMBr-CD PBS solution concentrations were combined with bacterial PBS suspensions containing 106-107 CFU/mL, and the mixtures were shaken at 37 °C. Diluted bacterial suspensions (100 μL, 1:1000) of the mixture at different time points (1, 2, 3, 4 h) were plated on LB solid medium. The sterilizing kinetic curve was generated by counting the bacterial colonies that had grown after 20-24 h of incubation.

A quantitative crystal violet assay was used to assess the ability of VOIMBr-CD to inhibit biofilm formation. 96-well plates were filled with a mixture of S. aureus and VOIMBr-CD solution at several concentrations (the final concentration of the bacterial suspension was 106 CFU/mL, and the final concentration of VOIMBr-CD was 0, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400 μg/mL). The 96-well plates were subjected to a 48 h incubation period at a temperature of 37 °C. Planktonic bacteria were then eliminated by carefully washing each well three times with PBS. The biofilm that developed on the well bottoms was treated with ethanol for 20 min to fix it. Subsequently, it was subjected to staining using a solution of 0.1 % crystal violet for a duration of 15 min and washed multiple times with PBS in order to eliminate excess dye. The biofilm inhibitory rate was then determined by measuring the OD590 after an equal volume of 30 % acetic acid was added to each well for dissolution. Additionally, the biofilm formed was also transferred to an EP tube containing 1 mL of PBS and vortexed vigorously for 5 min. After being gathered, the suspension was diluted 100 times with PBS and then was subsequently spread onto LB agar plates. Following an incubation period of 16-20 h at 37 °C, the colonies formed were photographed and recorded.

The capacity of VOIMBr-CD to disrupt the established biofilm was assessed through the crystal violet assay. S. aureus (with the final concentration of the bacterial suspension being 106 CFU/mL) was cultivated in LB medium for 48 h to obtain the biofilm, which was delicately washed three times with PBS for the removal of planktonic bacteria. Subsequently, it was combined with various concentrations of VOIMBr-CD solutions and added into a 96-well plate (the final concentrations were 0, 200, 400, 600, and 800 μg/mL). The 96-well plates were incubated in a 37 °C incubator for 24 h, and gently washed three times with PBS to eliminate the planktonic bacteria. The biofilm that formed at the bottom of the wells was fixed with ethanol for 20 min. Then it was stained with 0.1 % crystal violet for 15 min and rinsed several times with PBS to remove the excess dye. Eventually, an equal volume of 30 % acetic acid was added to each well for dissolution, and OD590 was measured to calculate the biofilm disruption rate.

S. aureus biofilms were then examined using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Coverslips that had been sterilized were inserted onto 96- or 12-well plates together with LB mixes of S. aureus (106-107 CFU/mL) and varying VOIMBr-CD concentrations. In order to eradicate the planktonic bacteria, the coverslips were subjected to three PBS washes following a 48 h incubation period at 37 °C. (1) After being fixed for 3h with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde, biofilms were dehydrated for 10 min using solutions of 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 90 %, and 100 % ethanol. Then the biofilms were observed by SEM. (2) Following staining with the Nucgreen/EthD III live and dead bacteria staining kit, the viable bacteria in the biofilm were measured by CLSM. Nucgreen, a green light-emitting dye, can stain bacterial cells with both intact and damaged surface structures (cell membranes); whereas EthD-III, a red light-emitting dye, can only stain bacterial cells with damaged surface structures. The use of two dyes at the same time can be an indicator of the survival status of bacteria [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of interactions between VOIMBr-CD and bacteria (E. Coli and S. aureus)
ce:section.ce:para: The binding ability of VOIMBr-CD to E. coli and S. aureus was investigated by measuring the change in Zeta potential before and after the incubation of VOIMBr-CD with the bacteria. 500 μL of VOIMBr-CD PBS solution, along with E. coli and S. aureus (OD600 = 0.1) were combined and incubated in a shaker for 3 h at 37 °C. Using centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min, the bacteria were recovered and then twice-washed in PBS. At room temperature, the Zeta potential was determined after the prepared bacteria were resuspended in 1 mL of PBS.

Furthermore, laser confocal images of E. coli and S. aureus after VOIMBr-CD treatment were examined. E. coli and S. aureus (OD600 = 0.1) were cocultured with 100 µg/mL of VOIMBr-CD PBS solution for 30 min and washed three times by centrifugation (5000 rpm, 5 min) in PBS. Bacterial imaging was performed using a laser confocal microscope at 100 × objective magnification.

S. aureus and E. coli (OD600 = 0.1) were cultivated with VOIMBr-CD (PBS solution) at 100 µg/mL for 2 h. 1.0-2.0 × 108 CFU/mL was the concentration of the bacterial suspension achieved by adding PBS again after the bacterial cells were centrifuged three times at 4 °C. Dilute staining solution: After thoroughly combining 1 µL NucGreen and 2 µL EthD III, 8 µL of PBS solution was added and mixed again. Then 10 µL of staining solution was added to 1 mL of bacterial solution (1.0-2.0 × 108 CFU/mL) and incubated for 15 min in the dark. The mixed solution was washed three times by centrifugation in PBS (5000 rpm, 10 min), then observed and photographed using CLSM.

The microscopic morphology of bacteria before and after VOIMBr-CD treatment was observed by field emission SEM. The bacterial PBS suspension with OD600 = 0.1 (approximately 1.5 × 108 CFU/mL) was co-incubated with VOIMBr-CD PBS solution (100 µg/mL) for 2 h, subsequently washed three times by PBS centrifugation (5000 rpm, 5 min). The fixation and dewatering procedures were consistent with the biofilm experiments. The dehydrated bacteria were dispersed in 100 % ethanol for morphological observation.

Differential ROS production in E. coli and S. aureus cells was assessed by fluorescence spectroscopy using DCFH-DA as a fluorescent probe. 500 μL of 106-107 CFU/mL E. coli and S. aureus were mixed with equal volumes of different concentrations of VOIMBr-CD PBS solutions (5, 10 and 20 μg/mL) and positive control (Rosup, 0.1 mM) by vortexing, followed by incubation for 3 h at 37 °C, 180 rpm in a light-protected shaker. After co-culture, bacterial cells were harvested from each EP tube by centrifugation (5000 rpm, 5 min) and washed 3 times with PBS. Under dark conditions, 1 mL DCFH-DA (10 μM) was added to the washed bacterial samples and cultured at 37 °C for 30 min. The fluorescence emission signal was detected using an excitation wavelength of 488 nm and the intracellular ROS level was proportional to the fluorescence intensity.

The research was conducted with ethical approval (No. LVRIAEC-2022–033) granted by the Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Lanzhou Veterinary Research Institute. The Blank, control, VOIMBr-CD and Van groups were created by randomly dividing twenty-four Kunming mice, aged six to eight weeks, into four groups. After administering anesthesia, a circular incision measuring approximately 1 cm in diameter was meticulously made on the dorsal region of the mice using surgical scissors. Subsequently, 100 μL of bacterial suspension (MRSA: 108 CFU/mL) was added by syringe to the formed wound site and then fixed with sterile medical gauze and tape for 24 h to infect the wound. The wound surface was bright red and emitted a certain amount of pus. One day following wound infection, one mouse from each group was sacrificed respectively for colony counting.

Every 24 h, the wounds of the Control, VOIMBr-CD and Van groups received 100 μL of PBS solution, VOIMBr-CD solution (100 μg/mL) and Van solution (100 μg/mL) for treatment. However, the blank group received no intervention. Following treatment, on days 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12, the mice's weight changes were noted and the wounds were photographed. And then the wound area was analyzed using ImageJ software. Mice were executed on day 12, and wound tissues were collected from four groups: 1. After fixation using paraformaldehyde solution (4 %), H&E staining, Masson staining, and immunohistochemical analysis were conducted; 2. wound tissues were homogenized in sterile saline, and homogenized tissue dilutions of different gradients were inoculated on LB agar medium and incubated for 20-24 h and colony counting was performed.

In addition, on day 12 of wound treatment, the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney) of four groups were isolated and fixed with paraformaldehyde solution (4 %) for H&E staining to evaluate the in vivo biocompatibility of VOIMBr-CD.

Cell activity was measured using the standard 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrabromozole (MTT) assay. Cervical cancer cells (HeLa, A549) at a density of 5000 cells/well were incubated in 96-well plates for 24 h. Each cell culture media was then supplemented with varying amounts of VOIMBr-CD or GTA-CDs. After 24 h of incubation, MTT solution was inserted into each well and incubation was continued for 4 h. After the medium was eliminated, 100 µL of DMSO were administered into each well. The well plates were gently vortexed to dissolve the precipitate formed and the absorbance was measured using a Bio-Rad model 680 ELISA.

Fresh erythrocytes from healthy mice were collected into centrifuge tubes containing 100 μL of sodium heparin. Fresh erythrocytes were centrifuged at 4 °C for 5 min (1200 rpm), the supernatant was removed, and then the erythrocytes were washed with sterile saline until no significant blood color was present in the supernatant. Afterwards, a 2 vol % suspension of erythrocytes was prepared in sterile saline. 100 μL of the resulting 2 vol % erythrocyte suspension was mixed with 100 μL of VOIMBr-CD or GTA-CDs saline solution at various concentrations to get the required concentration for the experiment, and then added to each well of a 96-well plate. Pure water was used as the positive control and normal saline as the negative control. The 96-well plate was incubated at 37 °C for 3 h. After incubation, the solution from each well was transferred to centrifuge tubes and centrifuged for 15 min (1200 rpm). The absorbance of the supernatant obtained was measured at 540 nm.
The following formula was used to calculate the hemolysis rate: Hemolysis = [OD540(VOIMBr-CD)-OD540(Negative)]/[OD540(Positive)-OD540(Negative)] × 100 %.

The results are reported as the average value plus or minus the standard deviation (SD). Statistical analysis of the data from each group was performed using the t-test. Statistical significance was defined as p < 0.05 (*) and p < 0.01 (**).

VOIMBr-CD were synthesized from GTA and VOIMBr by a simple one-pot method. The size distribution of CDs affects their antibacterial activity: the antibacterial activity increased with the decrease of CDs size [52]. The morphology, size distribution and dispersion of VOIMBr-CD were examined using TEM. As shown in Fig. 1
a, VOIMBr-CD were quasi-spherical with good dispersion. The size of VOIMBr-CD is small and uniformly distributed, with an average size of about 2.25 nm, which may be one of the reasons for the excellent antibacterial activity of VOIMBr-CD. The (100)-crystal plane of graphite is matched by CD crystal stripes with spacings of 0.21 nm in high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) pictures [53]. The (002) plane is indicated by the peak at 23° in the XRD pattern (Fig. 1 b). And the (100) plane is represented by the peak at 42°, which is in accordance with the measurements made in the HRTEM [54,55].

The properties of VOIMBr-CD are significantly influenced by the characteristics of their surface structure and chemical composition [39,56]. To explore the structure and chemical composition of VOIMBr-CD, the surface group retention of VOIMBr-CD was investigated by FT-IR, and the chemical bonding and elemental composition were revealed by XPS. As depicted in Fig. 1 c, the C-H stretching vibration peak of the alkene in VOIMBr at 3070 cm−1 was not observed for VOIMBr-CD. And the absence of the C-H stretching vibration peak around 1265 cm−1 in the FT-IR images of VOIMBr-CD suggests that the epoxy group and vinyl group underwent a ring-opening reaction, resulting in the formation of a C=O stretching vibration peak at 1718 cm−1 [46]. Furthermore, the C-H stretching vibration peaks of methyl and methylene at 2927 cm−1 and 2857 cm−1 as well as imidazolium-related peaks at 1650 cm−1 and 1160 cm−1 were retained [57,58]. The results demonstrate that the alkyl chain and positively charged imidazo group of VOIMBr were successfully retained in VOIMBr-CD through the ring-opening reaction of the vinyl group and epoxy group. The structures of VOIMBr and VOIMBr-CD were further compared using their XPS profiles. As shown in Fig. S2 a, VOIMBr contains three elements: carbon, nitrogen, and bromine. By examining the high-resolution C1s map (Fig. S2 b), two peaks measuring 284.5 eV and 285.7 eV were discovered, which represent the C-C/C=C bond and the C-N/C=N bond, respectively [59]. High-resolution N1s map analysis (Fig. S2 c) produced two peaks at 398.5 eV and 401.0 eV, respectively, which were assigned to imidazolium N and imidazolium N+ structures [60]. The XPS atlas confirmed VOIMBr's structure once more. The full spectrum of XPS (Fig. 1 d) indicated that VOIMBr-CD contained three elements, involving carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, with element contents of 82.78 %, 6.28 % and 10.94 %, respectively. The high-resolution C1s map (Fig. 1 e) displayed two fitting peaks at 284.7 and 285.9 eV, which are attributed to C-C/C=C and C-N/C-O bonds [61]. Analysis of the high-resolution N1s map (Fig. 1 f) revealed two signal peaks at 399.1 and 401.7 eV, corresponding to the C-N-C and quaternary N structures respectively [62]. Quaternary N+ structures encompass the quaternary ammonium structures of GTA and imidazolium N+ structures of VOIMBr. In the high-resolution O1s map (Fig. 1 g), two peaks were identified: 531.9 and 532.4 eV, which can be assigned to O=C-OH and O=C-N [43]. XPS and FT-IR results are consistent, both demonstrating that VOIMBr-CD retain the antibacterial structure of VOIMBr: alkyl chains and imidazolium groups, and concurrently retained the quaternary ammonium structure of GTA, which confer excellent antibacterial activity.

The FL spectrum and UV–vis absorption spectrum were utilized to examine the optical characteristics of VOIMBr-CD [42]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1 h) of VOIMBr-CD exhibited n → π∗ transition of C=O at 312 nm. The FL spectrum (Fig. 1 i) revealed that the optimum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were 420 nm and 500 nm, respectively. Meanwhile, the VOIMBr-CD solution was brown in visible light and fluoresced bright yellow-green under 365 nm UV light. The influence of different excitation wavelengths on the emission wavelength of VOIMBr-CD was then discussed. As shown in Fig. S3 a, the emission wavelength remained relatively stable despite varying the excitation wavelength within the range of 360 nm to 440 nm, with only a slight redshift. In addition, VOIMBr-CD had good photostability and their fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged after 120 min of continuous irradiation with a UV lamp (Fig. S3 b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial performance of VOIMBr-CD
ce:section.ce:para:
We examined the dose–response relationship of VOIMBr-CD towards Gram-negative bacteria, namely E. coli and P. aeruginosa, as well as Gram-positive bacteria, namely B. subtilis and S. aureus. Under the bacterial quantity of 106-107 CFU/mL: for E. coli (Fig. 2
a and S4), the sterilization rate of VOIMBr-CD could arrive at 63.29 % at the concentration of 25 μg/mL, and complete killing could be accomplished at the concentration of 100 μg/mL; for P. aeruginosa (Fig. 2 b and S4), the sterilization rate of VOIMBr-CD could achieve 60.63 % at the concentration of 50 μg/mL, and the sterilization rate could reach 84.53 % at the concentration of 400 μg/mL; for B. subtilis (Fig. 2 c and S4), the sterilization rate of VOIMBr-CD could reach 85.87 % at the concentration of 20 μg/mL, and full elimination was possible at the concentration of 40 μg/mL; for S. aureus (Fig. 2 d and S5), the sterilization rate of VOIMBr-CD could attain 77.02 % at the concentration of 2.5 μg/mL, and it could achieve complete killing at the concentration of 5 μg/mL. It can be concluded that the antibacterial effect of VOIMBr-CD against Gram-positive bacteria is conspicuously superior to that against Gram-negative bacteria, and it exhibits certain antibacterial selectivity towards Gram-positive bacteria. In the presence of varying strains, after enumerating the bacterial amount of distinct strains, we can approximately ascertain the dosage and frequency of VOIMBr-CD based on the dose-response relationship of VOIMBr-CD against different strains.

The selective antibacrerial activity of VOIMBr-CD was investigated using S. aureus and E. coli as typical organisms, employing the colony counting method. As shown in Fig. 2 d and Fig. S5 , the inhibitory ability of VOIMBr-CD against S. aureus exhibited a dose-dependent response; even at a low concentration of 5 μg/mL, VOIMBr-CD completely eradicated 1.5 × 106 CFU/mL of S. aureus, while no bactericidal effect was observed for E. coli (2.0 × 106 CFU/mL). At a concentration of 20 μg/mL, the survival rate of E. coli after treatment with VOIMBr-CD remained at 50 % (1.0 × 106 CFU/mL), indicating that the selective inactivation capability of VOIMBr-CD towards S. aureus (Gram-positive bacteria). To ascertain that synthetic VOIMBr-CD possess significantly enhanced bacterial inhibitory capacity compared to their raw materials (GTA and VOIMBr), their inhibitory efficiencies were compared using the standard plate counting method at an equivalent concentration (20 μg/mL). The inhibitory efficacy against S. aureus demonstrated by VOIMBr-CD surpassed that achieved by GTA and VOIMBr (Fig. 2 e and 2f). The superior bacterial suppressive ability of the synthetic VOIMBr-CD compared to the raw material may be related to the retention of the alkyl chain and the positively charged imidazolium group of VOIMBr.

The bacterial growth curves were further analyzed to assess the inhibitory effect of VOIMBr-CD on S. aureus (Fig. S6 a) and E. coli (Fig. S6 b). At a concentration of 20 μg/mL, VOIMBr-CD exhibited complete inhibition of S. aureus growth. As for E. coli, VOIMBr-CD at a concentration of 200 μg/mL also failed to inhibit its growth. These results once again demonstrate the selective bactericidal activity of VOIMBr-CD against S. aureus. The rapid bactericidal ability of VOIMBr-CD against S. aureus was evaluated using kinetic curves that measured the concentration changes over time following treatment with varying concentrations of VOIMBr-CD (Fig. 2 g). The kinetic curve revealed that at a concentration of 10 μg/mL, after 2 h of treatment with VOIMBr-CD, approximately 98 % of S. aureus was killed. Whereas, the killing efficiency reached 99.9 % after a 2 h treatment with a concentration of 20 μg/mL. To compare the bactericidal efficacy between VOIMBr-CD and commercially available antimicrobials (Jieyoushen antibacterial spray), we conducted experiments as shown in Fig. 2 h and Fig. S7 , which clearly illustrated that VOIMBr-CD are significantly more effective in killing bacteria than Jieyoushen antibacterial spray: at a concentration of 5 μg/mL, VOIMBr-CD achieved a 100 % bactericidal efficiency compared to only 21 % for Jieyoushen antibacterial spray. These findings highlight the superior selective killing potency and fast low-concentration bactericidal effect exhibited by VOIMBr-CD when compared to commercially available antibacterial drugs. As depicted in Table S1 , we conducted a comparison of the antibacterial efficacies of several nanomaterials (including metal nanomaterials, polymer nanomaterials, and other CDs) against S. aureus. The antibacterial effect of VOIMBr-CD is exceptionally remarkable. VOIMBr-CD is comparable to or even surpasses many advanced antibacterial agents and holds great application potential.

VOIMBr-CD exhibited exceptional antibacterial activity against S. aureus. Consequently, we conducted a further exploration on the antibacterial effect of VOIMBr-CD against multidrug-resistant bacteria-MRSA and made a comparison with traditional antibiotics. Van serves as the ultimate line of defense against MRSA. The antibacterial effect of VOIMBr-CD against MRSA surpasses Van. As shown in Fig. 2 i and S8, at a concentration of 20 μg/mL, VOIMBr-CD completely eradicated MRSA after 2 h of action, while the survival rate of MRSA remained at 31.48 % after 2 h of the effect of Van. The reason why VOIMBr-CD can overcome drug resistance might lie in their targeting and destructive capability towards the bacterial surface. VOIMBr-CD predominantly target the bacterial membrane through electrostatic interaction. The alkyl chains can infiltrate and disrupt the cell membrane structure due to their inherent hydrophobic effect, thereby inducing the generation of intracellular ROS in bacteria and resulting in bacterial death. Hence, as time progresses, bacteria may counteract the antibacterial effect of VOIMBr-CD via the following approaches: (1) Remodeling the envelope, such as modifying the thickness and composition of the cell wall; (2) Deactivating the nanomaterials, such as the aggregation and degradation of nanomaterials. Once bacteria adapt to nanomaterials and acquire drug resistance, VOIMBr-CD need to be re-engineered to restore the antibacterial function. The following methods could be employed to re-design VOIMBr-CD: (1) Preparing new IL-CD by altering the anion structure or antibacterial framework of the original IL; (2) Further modifying the responsive functional molecules on the surface of VOIMBr-CD, such as pH response, photothermal response, and enzymatic response; (3) Grafting targeting ligands on the surface of VOIMBr-CD for precise sterilization, such as certain polysaccharides or proteins with recognition capabilities [63].

Finally, we examined the antibacterial stability of the PBS solution of VOIMBr-CD when stored at 4 °C. As shown in the Fig. S9 , the antibacterial efficacy of the VOIMBr-CD solution started to show a slight decline after 30 days of storage, whereas no significant alteration was observed in the antibacterial efficacy after 17 days of storage. Consequently, to better retain the antibacterial functionality of VOIMBr-CD, the VOIMBr-CD solution ought to be freshly prepared after being stored at 4 °C for 30 days before utilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-biofilm activity of VOIMBr-CD
ce:section.ce:para:
Bacterial biofilms, which pose a challenge for antimicrobial agents to penetrate, often serve as the origin of induced resistance and can lead to chronic bacterial infections that are arduous to treat [64]. On the basis of the selective inhibition of S. aureus by VOIMBr-CD, we further investigated the ability of VOIMBr-CD to prevent the development of S. aureus biofilm formation. The extent of inhibition was quantified using crystal violet staining. As shown in Fig. 3
a and 3b, the inhibitory rate of VOIMBr-CD on S. aureus biofilm rose with increasing concentration. The inhibitory rate of 400 μg/mL VOIMBr-CD on S. aureus biofilm reached 90.72 %, while the concentration below 200 μg/mL VOIMBr-CD showed no obvious inhibitory effect on S. aureus biofilm. The results of the dilution plating after biofilm vortex transfer are also consistent with the results of crystal violet staining (Fig. 3 c). The reason why the inhibitory concentration of biofilm growth is above 200 μg/mL might lie in the poor dispersibility of VOIMBr-CD in the LB medium compared to that in PBS, and it can exert the antibacterial effect only at a relatively higher concentration. As shown in Table S1 , compared with other nanomaterials, the ability of VOIMBr-CD to inhibit biofilm growth can be classified as at a medium level.

The morphology and structure of the biofilm were observed using SEM, while bacterial survival within the biofilm was determined through CLSM. As shown in Fig. 3 d, the untreated control group exhibited a dense and mature biofilm with robust bacterial growth, resulting in almost complete bacterial survival. However, upon treatment with VOIMBr-CD at a concentration of 300 μg/mL, there was a noticeable reduction in bacterial adhesion along with loosening of the biofilm structure, leading to a significant decrease in surviving colonies. Furthermore, treatment with VOIMBr-CD at a concentration of 400 μg/mL resulted in near-complete eradication of the biofilm, leaving only isolated bacteria capable of survival. These findings unequivocally demonstrate that VOIMBr-CD possess remarkable efficacy as inhibitors against S. aureus biofilms and hold great promise as potential therapeutic agents.

The in vitro antibacterial assays have demonstrated the selective inhibitory effect of VOIMBr-CD on Gram-positive bacteria, specifically S. aureus. In this work, the selective inhibition mechanism of VOIMBr-CD was investigated in detail from several perspectives. The cell wall composition of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria differs significantly. In Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus), the cell wall primarily consists of a monolayer structure composed of peptidoglycan, along with anionic components such as lipoteichoic acids and teichoic acids. This relatively loose structure facilitates easier binding of positively charged VOIMBr-CD to the bacterial surface. The cell wall of Gram-negative bacteria is a relatively compact double-layered structure, encompassing peptidoglycan and an outer membrane. The outer membrane is predominantly composed of lipoproteins, lipid bilayers, and lipopolysaccharides (LPS) [36,65]. LPS exerts a protective influence on bacterial cell membranes, consisting of lipid A, which encompasses negatively charged phosphate groups and can augment the negative surface charge of bacteria as well as stabilize the structure of the cell membrane. Ibsen et al. verified through molecular dynamics that negatively charged phosphate groups at the head of lipid A bind to positively charged choline, and choline is trapped outside the compact lipid bilayer and is unable to diffuse inward [66]. Thus, we conjecture that when in contact with Gram-negative bacteria, positively charged VOIMBr-CD bind to the negatively charged part of lipid A, trapping VOIMBr-CD outside the lipid bilayer and preventing them from entering bacterial cells to inflict effective damage. The loose monolayer membrane surface structure of Gram-positive bacteria is more conducive to VOIMBr-CD penetration than the dense bilayer membrane surface structure of Gram-negative bacteria. The selective inhibitory activity of VOIMBr-CD may be due to their different surface binding forces with Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

To verify the above conclusions, the binding of VOIMBr-CD to the bacterial surface was investigated by Zeta potential changes. As shown in Fig. 4
a, the Zeta potentials of S. aureus and E. coli were −14.25 mV and −15.50 mV respectively, confirming that both bacteria have a negative surface charge; VOIMBr-CD had a Zeta potential of 10.7 mV and a positive surface charge, probably because VOIMBr-CD retained the imidazolium moiety of VOIMBr. The Zeta potential of both S. aureus and E. coli treated with VOIMBr-CD became positive at 4.93 mV and 2.28 mV respectively, indicating that VOIMBr-CD were able to bind to S. aureus and E. coli through electrostatic interaction. In contrast, the Zeta potential of S. aureus was slightly more positive than E. coli, probably because more VOIMBr-CD were bound to the S. aureus surface. Based on the fluorescence emission capability of VOIMBr-CD, the attachment of the same concentration of VOIMBr-CD to the surface of both bacteria was further assessed by CLSM. At 488 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the S. aureus system treated with VOIMBr-CD was much stronger than the E. coli system treated with VOIMBr-CD (Fig. 4 b). This indicated that VOIMBr-CD had a significant difference in adhesion to the two bacteria, with stronger adhesion to S. aureus. These findings all point to VOIMBr-CD attaching to S. aureus surfaces more easily.

The SEM technique was employed to investigate the alterations in bacterial morphology (S. aureus and E. coli) pre- and post-treatment with VOIMBr-CD. As shown in Fig. 4 c, both S. aureus and E. coli untreated with VOIMBr-CD had smooth surface structures. The surface of S. aureus cells was significantly raised, wrinkled and rough after treatment with VOIMBr-CD, indicating that VOIMBr- CD can further penetrate and destroy the surface structure of S. aureus through the hydrophobic action of the alkyl chain after binding to S. aureus through electrostatic action [41]. In contrast, the surface of E. coli cells did not change significantly after treatment with VOIMBr-CD, demonstrating that VOIMBr-CD had little ability to damage the surface of E. coli cells. Additionally, the staining experiment of live and dead bacteria can also be used to study the damage to the surface structure of the two bacteria under the action of VOIMBr-CD. As shown in Fig. 4 d, untreated S. aureus and E. coli only fluoresced green and the bacterial cells survived well. After treatment with VOIMBr-CD, S. aureus showed only red fluorescence in the EthD III channel, suggesting that most of the S. aureus died due to cell membrane rupture. With the exception of a small number of bacteria that fluoresced red, the majority of E. coli treated with VOIMBr-CD were living bacteria with intact cell membranes. Both SEM and live-dead bacterial staining experiments again demonstrated the selective inhibitory activity of VOIMBr-CD: under the same conditions, VOIMBr-CD were able to destroy the cell surface structure of most S. aureus, while the cell surface structure of most E. coli remained intact.

In addition to electrostatic and hydrophobic effects, the increase in ROS levels in bacterial cells is also an important mechanism leading to bacterial death [67–69]. Bacterial intracellular ROS levels were assessed using the ROS Assay Kit. Rosup is a positive control that converts non-fluorescent DCFH-DA to fluorescent dichlorofluorescein (DCF) in bacterial suspensions. DCFH-DA is a commonly used probe for ROS. DCFH-DA is converted to DCF in the presence of ROS and the fluorescence intensity is proportional to the level of ROS [44]. As demonstrated in Fig. S11
a, following treatment with 5 μg/mL VOIMBr-CD, S. aureus generated 9.90 % ROS compared with the positive control, and the amount of ROS generation rose as the concentration of VOIMBr-CD increased. S. aureus produced 52.28 % ROS after treatment with 20 μg/mL of VOIMBr-CD. In contrast, E. coli ( Fig. S11
b) only produced 18.48 % ROS upon treatment with 20 μg/mL of VOIMBr-CD. Thus, VOIMBr-CD was able to induce intracellular ROS production in bacteria, which lead to bacterial death. The active oxygen level of at least 10 % proves beneficial for antibacterial activity; whereas for the complete elimination of bacteria, the active oxygen level needs to reach 50 %. Perhaps by investigating the dose–response relationship of ROS generation elicited by IL-CDs of diverse structures, it could furnish a basis for the design and optimization of materials, and it is feasible to obtain IL-CDs with more superior antibacterial activity.

Based on the above discussion and analysis, we hypothesize that the mechanism of selective bacterial inhibition by VOIMBr-CD may be as follows: First, due to electrostatic interactions and differences in the composition of the cell walls of Gram-positive and negative bacteria, positively charged VOIMBr-CD bind more readily to the surface of Gram-positive bacteria-S. aureus, which has an anionic component (lipoteichoic acids and teichoic acids) and a relatively loose structure. Upon binding to the bacterial surface, the long alkyl chains on the surface of VOIMBr-CD interact deep into the cell membrane through hydrophobic interactions, which has the potential to result in harm to the membrane and an elevation in its permeability. Finally, VOIMBr-CD enters the interior of the bacteria and induces the production of ROS, resulting in bacterial death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antibacterial performance of VOIMBr-CD
ce:section.ce:para:
Bacterial infection is a significant contributor to the development of chronic wounds, and bacterial invasion can considerably impede wound healing, leading to further infectious complications [70]. Based on the good killing effect of VOIMBr-CD on MRSA, we developed an experimental model using skin excision wounds infected with MRSA to assess the in vivo efficacy of VOIMBr-CD in combating bacterial growth. A skin wound measuring approximately 10 mm in diameter was subjected to a 24 h exposure of S. aureus, resulting in a bright red wound surface with pus exudation (Fig. 5
a). Representative images of the wound at various time points were presented in Fig. 5 b as indicated. We assessed wound healing rate (Fig. 5 c) as indicators of wound healing progress among different groups. It is notable that, in contrast to other groups, the wounds treated with VOIMBr-CD exhibited significantly accelerated healing. After 12 days of treatment, the wound healing rate of the VOIMBr-CD treatment group could attain 98.89 %, which was conspicuously higher than the blank group (86.60 %), the control group (85.71 %), and the Van group (92.98 %). To explore the in vivo bactericidal efficacy of VOIMBr-CD, the colonies of wound tissues in four groups were enumerated on the 0th and 12th days of wound treatment. As depicted in the Fig. S12 , on the 0th day of treatment, the results of dilution plate coating and colony counting revealed that the magnitudes of wound colonies in four groups were all at 108 CFU/mL. Twelve days post-treatment, the quantity of surviving bacteria in the blank group and the control group was two magnitudes higher than that in the VOIMBr-CD group; whereas the number of surviving bacteria in the Van group was one magnitude higher than that in the VOIMBr-CD group (Fig. 5 d and 5e). This implies that VOIMBr-CD exhibits the most superior in vivo bactericidal activity. It is notable that, as depicted in Table S2 , in contrast to the prevailing wound dressings, the efficacy of VOIMBr-CD in treating MRSA-infected wounds is satisfactory. Furthermore, throughout the entire treatment process, the weight of the mice increased almost steadily, suggesting that VOIMBr-CD is safe for treatment and does not cause significant toxicity (Fig. 5 f).

Subsequently, three indicators associated with the wound healing process were examined through H&E staining, Masson staining, and immunohistochemical analysis, including inflammatory factors, collagen deposition, and angiogenesis. As demonstrated in Fig. 6
a, epidermal damage, edema, hemorrhage, as well as inflammatory cell infiltration were all detectable in the blank group, control group, and Van group; contrarily, the epidermal layer in the VOIMBr-CD treatment group was intact, the dermal tissue was markedly more organized, edema and hemorrhage were significantly alleviated, no inflammatory cell infiltration was noted, and new hair follicles emerged [36]. H&E staining revealed that the treatment of VOIMBr-CD could attenuate the inflammatory response and facilitate the formation of new tissues. It can be discerned by analyzing the collagen deposition in Masson staining (Fig. 6 f), the expression levels of the two inflammatory factors IL-6 (Fig. 6 g) and TNF-α (Fig. 6 h), and the expression level of the VEGF (Fig. 6 i) angiogenic factor that compared with the other three groups, the VOIMBr-CD treatment group can more effectively promote collagen deposition, diminish the expression of inflammatory factors, facilitate angiogenesis, and expedite the wound healing process effectively. Therefore, VOIMBr-CD can exert an antibacterial role in vivo, mitigate the expression of inflammatory factors, enhance collagen deposition and angiogenesis, and serves as a promising MRSA-infected wound dressing. Nevertheless, VOIMBr-CD fall short of other properties conducive to wound healing, such as antioxidation. In future endeavors, it is hoped that we can design wound dressings that possess both exceptional antibacterial activity and antioxidation capacity.

The in vitro biocompatibility of VOIMBr-CD was assessed through cytotoxicity assay and hemolysis assay. The cytotoxicity and hemolytic activity of VOIMBr-CD were concentration-dependent and rose with the increase in the concentration of VOIMBr-CD. As depicted in Fig. 7
a and 7b, VOIMBr-CD exhibited excellent biocompatibility at concentrations under 100 μg/mL. At a concentration of 100 μg/mL, the cell survival rate after VOIMBr-CD treatment exceeded 90 %, and the hemolysis rate was merely 3.69 %. Generally, nanomaterials exhibiting a hemolysis rate not exceeding 5 % are considered to possess good hemocompatibility [36]. At a concentration of 500 μg/mL, the cell survival rate after VOIMBr-CD treatment was above 70 %, and the hemolysis rate was 10.99 %. Nevertheless, at a concentration of 800 μg/mL, the cell survival rate after VOIMBr-CD treatment was 55.51 %, and the hemolysis rate was 12.97 %. As depicted in Table S1 , in contrast to other materials, VOIMBr-CD exhibits certain cytotoxicity and a hemolysis rate at concentrations exceeding 100 μg/mL. Its biocompatibility is insufficiently favorable, yet the degree of hemolysis is not significant. Van does not induce cytotoxicity at a high concentration of 8 mg/mL, and its biocompatibility is superior to VOIMBr-CD [71]. However, the antibacterial efficacy of VOIMBr-CD was relatively favorable. The concentration of the wound healing experiment was merely 100 μg/mL, without obvious cytotoxicity and hemolytic activity. The H&E staining outcomes of mouse organs demonstrated that subsequent to the treatment with VOIMBr-CD, there was scarcely any pathological impairment in the crucial organs of mice (Fig. 7 c). This once again indicates that VOIMBr-CD at a concentration of 100 μg/mL is both safe and effective for treating wounds infected with MRSA. Consequently, VOIMBr-CD possessed excellent in vivo biocompatibility. Additionally, we probed into the in vitro biocompatibility of GTA-CD. Firstly, the successful preparation of GTA-CD was affirmed by TEM ( Fig. S13 ). In contrast to VOIMBr-CD, the biocompatibility of GTA-CD is superior. At a concentration of 800 μg/mL, the cell survival rate exceeded 70 %, and the hemolytic activity was 7.64 % ( Fig. S14 ). This might be ascribed to the alkyl chain structure introduced by VOIMBr, which could potentially exert a certain level of toxicity to cells.

VOIMBr-CD has moderate cytotoxicity at high concentrations. Consequently, it is conceivable to combine VOIMBr-CD with other antibacterial drugs to generate a synergistic effect, further enhance antibacterial activity and biocompatibility, and achieve superior application performance. However, the most critical point lies in which method to adopt to enable the combination of the two to exert a synergistic effect. Perhaps in the subsequent work, we can undertake further exploration.

In summary, we developed and synthesized novel VOIMBr-CD as antibacterial agents with enhanced activity through a rapid and straightforward method. Compared with van, VOIMBr-CD manifested superior antibacterial efficacy against MRSA. The antibacterial mechanism of VOIMBr-CD was elucidated by considering the interplay of electrostatic interactions, surface composition disparities between Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, hydrophobic effects, and ROS generation. In an animal model of MRSA-infected wounds, VOIMBr-CD effectively eradicated MRSA at the wound site, facilitated collagen deposition and angiogenesis, and concurrently expedited wound healing. There was no organ toxicity after wound treatment, thereby emphasizing their potential as candidate drugs for the treatment of diseases analogous to MRSA infections.
